← Back to Sydney Soon — Sydney
Fiddletown

Hornsby Shire

NSW 2159

Darug Country

Character

Walking down the "main street" of Fiddletown is a misnomer — there isn't one. Instead of shopfronts and kerbs, you find long, winding gravel driveways and post-and-rail fences. The atmosphere is defined by the scent of eucalyptus and the rhythmic crunch of tyres on unsealed roads. In the morning, the rhythm is dictated by the sun and the local birds; evenings are pitch black, offering some of the best stargazing in the Greater Sydney area.

The "reputation" of the suburb is formed entirely by private landmarks — luxury equestrian estates and hidden orchards. There are no iconic cafes or bars. The social life happens at property gates or over post-and-rail fences. It is quiet, private, and dominated by the sounds of the bush.

 

The Paradox: Fiddletown is technically part of "Greater Sydney," yet it feels more like the Blue Mountains or the Hunter Valley. Residents are only 20 minutes from the suburban bustle of Hornsby, yet they live in a zone where mobile reception can still be a hit-or-miss adventure.

It sits on a high plateau of Hawkesbury Sandstone — the soil so unique that it supports specific rare flora found nowhere else in the Sydney basin. It is one of Sydney's smallest suburbs by population, frequently disappearing from standard urban data sets. If you are there, you meant to go there: Fiddletown is, essentially, a cul-de-sac suburb.

45
Distance from CBD
45km
Approximately 55–70 minutes by private vehicle via the M2 or NorthConnex. No train, no ferry, no light rail. Life without a car is not merely inconvenient — it is impossible.
Top
Price percentile
Top 10%
Median acreage estate: $2,850,000 (+22% since 2021). No units exist. Top percentile for land size-to-price ratio in Sydney. Gross rental yield 2.4% — a lifestyle market, not an investment play.
48
Median age
48yrs
Population approximately 160 people. Only 8% live alone — mostly family households. Average tenure 14 years. People who come tend to stay.
259
Named, contested, survived
259 res.
In 2003, locals fought off a council merger that would have erased the Fiddletown name. Named as a joke in the 1890s after three farmers who played violin in their huts. Still not merging with Arcadia.
A hidden sanctuary of rustic acreage where the Sydney skyline is a distant memory and the silence of the bush is the local soundtrack — technically Greater Sydney, spiritually the Hunter Valley.

Safety
Who lives here

The Equestrian Elite

Wealthy Professionals & Horse Owners

Work in the city; live for their horses. They chose Fiddletown for the acreage and direct trail access into Marramarra National Park. The morning routine involves horses, not baristas.

The Legacy Farmers

Multi-Generation Land Holders

Families who have held land for generations — maintaining small-scale orchards or hobby farms. Some of the original sandstone miner orchards still producing wild fruit inside the National Park border.

The Stealth Wealth Recluse

High-Net-Worth Individuals

Total privacy away from corporate noise or paparazzi. Architecturally designed homes integrated into the bush. Gates that do not open for Google Street View. The real estate agent is sworn to silence.

The Nature Purist

Eco-Conscious Families

Sustainable, architect-designed homes integrated into the bush. Starlink on the roof, rainwater tank under it. 42% hold higher education. They moved here before "off-grid" became an aesthetic.

Private estate — tennis court at dusk
Sunset terrace — bush views to the horizon
Heritage cottage and kitchen garden
The estate gates at dusk
Lakeside living — nature at every window
Off-grid waterfront retreat

Life in Fiddletown

2026
01

No Main Street

There isn't one. Instead of shopfronts and kerbs: long gravel driveways, post-and-rail fences, and the occasional horse float. Social life happens at property gates or over the fence. The "community hub" is the local RFS fire brigade.

02

Food & Drink

Every day — requires driving to Galston or Dural for essentials. Upscale — The Local Trade at Dural. The reality: residents grow much of their own food. The orchard at the back of the property is not decorative.

03

Nature Superpower

Direct access to Marramarra National Park — world-class bushwalking and rugged mountain biking. The National Park is not a weekend destination; it is the backyard. Some properties back directly onto it.

04

Stargazing

No streetlights. None. The Milky Way is visible year-round with a clarity that shocks first-time visitors. Residents do not regard this as unusual. They regard sodium streetlights as a planning failure visited upon other places.

05

The School Run

A 40-minute round trip. Every single day. The nearest primary is regional; secondary requires serious logistics. This is the trade. Residents know the trade before they sign. Most consider it still favourable.

06

2026 Update

The Fiddletown Fire Protection Upgrade completed — new high-capacity water tanks for the RFS. Strict new Green Overlay zoning: large blocks cannot be subdivided. Fiddletown is resisting suburbanisation. It is working.

Resident Voices

01 — Horse Owner
54
Loves it
I can ride for hours without seeing a car. The trails into Marramarra are mine. Not metaphorically — I have not seen another person on them in eleven years.
Frustrates
People from the city complain about the lack of streetlights. That's the whole point. If you need streetlights, you live somewhere else.
02 — Remote Tech Executive
41
Loves it
The air is five degrees cooler than Parramatta. I bought a Starlink the week they launched. I have never gone back to the office. Not once.
Frustrates
The school run. Forty minutes, every day, each way. I have done it 1,200 times. I have accepted it the way one accepts rain.
03 — Retiree (14 years)
67
Loves it
I love the silence. Full silence. Not quiet — actual silence, the kind where you can hear a branch fall three paddocks over.
Frustrates
It takes a delivery driver forty-five minutes to find my gate. Last Christmas the turkey arrived on Boxing Day. I have now put the coordinates in my address.

2026 Update

Holding the Line Against Suburbanisation

Fiddletown is resisting the creep that has overtaken nearby Box Hill and Marsden Park. The new Green Overlay zoning makes subdivision of large blocks nearly impossible, locking in the acreage character. Combined with Starlink satellite internet finally making remote work genuinely seamless, the suburb is attracting a new wave of high-income buyers who want the silence and the bandwidth simultaneously.

Fiddletown Fire Protection Upgrade — Completed 2025
New high-capacity water tanks for the Rural Fire Service completed. The RFS brigade is active and locally embedded — a genuine community institution, not a response service from elsewhere.
Green Overlay Zoning — Announced 2026
Strict new zoning laws prevent the subdivision of large blocks. Fiddletown resists suburbanisation by legal instrument. The character of the plateau is now protected in planning law, not merely by distance.
Starlink Integration — Ongoing
High-speed satellite internet is finally making remote work seamless. The last barrier to full-time acreage living — patchy mobile and broadband — is being resolved, broadening the buyer profile.
